coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent sensor based on collagen fiber-derived carbon quantum dots and carboxymethylated nanocellulose for Fe3+ detection

coredata.dc:description:
Detection of Fe3+ is essential for environmental and food safety monitoring. However, conventional detection methods often require costly equipment and lengthy processes, limiting their on-site applications. To address these challenges, a fluorescent sensor was developed using carboxymethylated nanocellulose (CMCNF) and collagen fiber-based carbon quantum dots (CCQDs). The CCQDs, with a fluorescence quantum yield of 25.04 %, were synthesized via a microwave hydrothermal method using secondary collagen fibers. A selective and sensitive CMCNF/CCQDs sensor was fabricated by incorporating CCQDs into a CMCNF matrix through a simple solution-casting method. The CMCNF matrix ensured uniform dispersion and stability of the CCQDs, optimizing sensor performance. The sensor exhibited a linear detection range for Fe3+ between 0 μM and 180 μM, with a corresponding linear equation of y = 0.6496 + 0.3532 x
R 2
= 0.9932
. The limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was determined to be 3.84 μM. Excellent detection performance was demonstrated in real water samples, achieving recoveries from 99.45 % to 105.23 % and an RSD below 2 %. The fluorescence quenching mechanism underlying CCQDs and Fe3+ interactions was systematically explored. This study provides a sustainable strategy for the high-value utilization of biomass waste and offers a portable approach for Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent sensor based on collagen fiber-derived carbon quantum dots and carboxymethylated nanocellulose for Fe3+ detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Detection of Fe3+ is essential for environmental and food safety monitoring. However, conventional detection methods often require costly equipment and lengthy processes, limiting their on-site applications. To address these challenges, a fluorescent sensor was developed using carboxymethylated nanocellulose (CMCNF) and collagen fiber-based carbon quantum dots (CCQDs). The CCQDs, with a fluorescence quantum yield of 25.04 %, were synthesized via a microwave hydrothermal method using secondary collagen fibers. A selective and sensitive CMCNF/CCQDs sensor was fabricated by incorporating CCQDs into a CMCNF matrix through a simple solution-casting method. The CMCNF matrix ensured uniform dispersion and stability of the CCQDs, optimizing sensor performance. The sensor exhibited a linear detection range for Fe3+ between 0 μM and 180 μM, with a corresponding linear equation of y = 0.6496 + 0.3532 x
R 2
= 0.9932
. The limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ was determined to be 3.84 μM. Excellent detection performance was demonstrated in real water samples, achieving recoveries from 99.45 % to 105.23 % and an RSD below 2 %. The fluorescence quenching mechanism underlying CCQDs and Fe3+ interactions was systematically explored. This study provides a sustainable strategy for the high-value utilization of biomass waste and offers a portable approach for Fe3+ detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ plays essential roles in maintaining normal physiological functions, monitoring environmental quality, and ensuring food safety. As an essential trace element, Fe3+ is involved in various physiological processes, including oxygen transport, cellular metabolism, and enzyme function [1,2]. Deficiency or overload of iron can lead to serious health issues, such as anemia, immune dysfunction, iron toxicity, and cardiovascular diseases [3–5]. In the field of environmental science, Fe3+ is a crucial indicator of water quality and pollution [6]. Excessive accumulation of Fe3+ in the environment is often attributed to industrial wastewater discharges and mining activities. Therefore, developing effective and sensitive methods for Fe3+ detection is of great importance for early diagnosis, disease prevention, and environmental monitoring. Although several Fe3+ detection techniques have been developed, including optical methods [7], capillary electrophoresis [8], electrochemical methods [9], atomic absorption spectrometry [10], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [11], most of them suffer from limitations such as high instrumental cost, complex operation, and time consumption [12].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are ideal candidates for Fe3+ detection due to their optical stability, low toxicity, tunable surface properties, and high sensitivity [13,14]. Upon exposure to Fe3+, CQDs undergo fluorescence quenching, with the degree of quenching directly proportional to the ion concentration, making them highly sensitive and environmentally friendly for detection. Several studies have demonstrated the effectiveness of CQDs in Fe3+ detection: Shen et al. [15] synthesized BCQDs from sweet potatoes capable of detecting Fe3+ in a concentration range from 1 μM to 100 μM with a detection limit of 0.32 μM. Wu et al. [16] used zinc gluconate-derived blue fluorescent CQDs to detect both Fe3+ and lysine. However, CQDs tend to aggregate in aqueous solutions, leading to fluorescence quenching and reduced sensitivity. Ji et al. [17] studied the photoluminescence of CQDs in their aggregated state, showing that hydrogen bond-mediated self-assembly can modulate their emission. Despite these insights, maintaining stability and performance in aqueous environments remains a challenge. One promising solution is integrating CQDs into solid substrates, which can effectively mitigate aggregation and ensure long-term stability for reliable Fe3+ detection. Additionally, with the growing trend of miniaturization and portable devices for metal ion detection, Qiu et al. [18] developed hydrogel-based visual sensors using biomass-derived carbon dots for on-site Fe3+ detection via pseudo-color mapping, while their earlier work [19] involved immobilizing solvent-extracted carbon dots in agarose hydrogels for semi-quantitative Cr(VI) sensing. However, hydrogel sensors have limitations, including low mechanical strength and semi-quantitative analysis, which hinder precise Fe3+ detection and long-term stability. Research on portable carbon quantum dot sensors with high mechanical strength and long-term stability for precise quantitative Fe3+ detection remains limited.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from biomass resources, such as goose feathers [20], bee pollen [21], and coccinia grandis [22], have garnered significant attention due to their wide availability and environmentally friendly preparation methods [23–25]. Despite their potential, most biomass-derived CQDs face challenges, including low fluorescence quantum yields and complex synthesis processes. Nitrogen doping has proven to be an effective strategy to enhance the quantum yield and fluorescence properties of CQDs [26]. Leather, composed of collagen fibers [27], is a major source of waste in the leather industry, which generates over 600,000 tons of leather waste annually [28]. This includes chrome-containing collagen waste, which poses significant environmental risks [29]. About 80 % of tannery solid waste consists of secondary collagen fibers [30,31], but their use as raw materials remains underexplored. Nitrogen-rich secondary collagen fibers can be used to prepare collagen-derived carbon quantum dots (CCQDs). This enables nitrogen self-doping to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield and promotes waste recycling. Recent studies have demonstrated the feasibility of synthesizing CQDs from collagen waste for Fe3+ detection [32]. However, the utilization of chrome-containing leather waste remains unexplored. Microwave hydrothermal synthesis is a promising method for synthesizing CQDs. Compared to traditional hydrothermal methods, it offers distinct advantages in preparing CQDs. Microwave heating accelerates the reaction process by interacting with molecules in the solution via radiation, reducing energy consumption. It also enables precise control over reaction conditions, ensuring consistent and high-quality CQDs.

Cellulose is one of the most sustainable and biocompatible polymer materials worldwide [26,33], and its derivatives serve as excellent composite substrates due to their biodegradability, mechanical strength, and compatibility [34]. Nanocellulose-based composites are transparent, lightweight, and flexible, enabling diverse applications in packaging, biomedicine, sensors, flexible substrates, and printed electronics [35,36]. Junka et al. [37] synthesized fluorescent nanopapers with tunable fluorescence by coupling carbon dots with carboxymethylated nanocellulose (CMCNF) through an EDC/NHS reaction. You et al. [38] created a solar light conversion film by dispersing carbon dots into a carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) solution, demonstrating excellent mechanical properties and efficient UV-to-blue light conversion. The hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on CMCNF surfaces form strong hydrogen bonds with the amino groups of CQDs, ensuring uniform dispersion and enhancing the mechanical properties of the composite. CMCNF's network structure prevents CQDs aggregation, reducing fluorescence quenching and maintaining fluorescence performance. Hence, CMCNF is a promising matrix for stabilizing CQDs and enhancing sensor performance.

In this study, we developed a portable fluorescent sensor, CMCNF/CCQDs, using CCQDs with high fluorescence quantum yield as a probe and CMCNF as a carrier for Fe3+ detection in environmental and food packaging water. The CCQDs were synthesized from secondary collagen fibers via a simple and efficient microwave hydrothermal method. CMCNF was combined with CCQDs through a solution-casting method to ensure sensor stability. The micromorphology, structure, and properties of the obtained CCQDs and CMCNF/CCQDs were characterized, and their feasibility for Fe3+ detection was evaluated. We also investigated the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CCQDs by Fe3+. This study provides a green and simple strategy for high-value utilization of biomass waste and portable Fe3+ detection, showing great potential for environmental monitoring and food packaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Deionized water was prepared in the laboratory. Calcium oxide (CaO) and chloroacetic acid (MCA, 98 %) were obtained from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Potassium bromide (KBr) was obtained from Tianjin Kemo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Secondary collagen fibers were supplied by Guangdong Wuyuan New Material Technology Group Co., Ltd. PBS buffer solution (pH 7.2–7.4) was obtained from Hunan Bikman Holding Co., Ltd. Bleached sulfate softwood pulp was supplied by Canfor Canada. Sulfuric acid (98 % AR), anhydrous ethanol, and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from National Pharmaceutical Reagent Co., Ltd. Quinine sulfate and a series of metal salts, including AgNO3, CuSO4·5H2O, CdCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, ZnCl2, NiCl2, PbSO4, HgSO4, FeSO4·7H2O, and AlCl3·6H2O, were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations
Preparation of secondary collagen fiber-derived carbon quantum dots (CCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Chromium was removed from secondary collagen fibers, and collagen was hydrolyzed using the alkaline method. A mixture of 20 g secondary collagen fibers, 1.6 g calcium oxide, and 480 g deionized water was added to a flask and reacted at 60 °C for 5 h in a constant temperature water bath. Afterward, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was collected to obtain the hydrolyzed collagen solution. 50 mL of the hydrolyzed collagen solution was added to a PTFE digestion tank and subjected to microwave hydrothermal reaction using a microwave digestion instrument (FUP Trp8, Qingdao Yizhuo Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd., China). The reaction was carried out at various temperatures (150 °C, 170 °C, 190 °C, 210 °C, 230 °C) and times (20 min, 40 min, 60 min, 80 min, 100 min) in single-factor experiments to evaluate the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the CCQDs. Based on the results, a two-factor, three-level orthogonal experiment was conducted to optimize the reaction conditions by varying reaction time and temperature (Table S1). After the reaction, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm ultrafiltration membrane and dialyzed in deionized water for 7 days using a 1000 Da dialysis bag, with water changes every 12 h. The dialysate was lyophilized to obtain CCQDs powders.

Chromium (Cr) content in the secondary collagen fibers and hydrolyzed collagen solution was quantified using an ICS-900 ion chromatograph. The Cr removal rate was calculated using Eq. (1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carboxymethylated nanocellulose (CMCNF)
ce:section.ce:para: Carboxymethylated nanocellulose (CMCNF) was prepared through carboxymethylation modification combined with mechanical processing. A 5 g sample of bleached sulfate softwood pulp (dry mass), 135 mL anhydrous ethanol, 15 mL deionized water, 13.6 g/L NaOH, and 15 g/L chloroacetic acid (MCA) were added to a three-necked flask and mixed. The mixture was heated to 90 °C and refluxed for 50 min. The slurry was washed with deionized water until neutral pH was achieved, then homogenized to obtain CMCNF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensor (CMCNF/CCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensors (CMCNF/CCQDs) were prepared using the solution-casting method. CMCNF and CCQDs were mixed in the proportions listed in Table 1 and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was degassed under vacuum, poured into Petri dishes, and dried in a constant-temperature oven at 40 °C and 60 % relative humidity (RH) for 48 h. The films were labeled based on CCQD content, from CMCNF/CCQDs-0 to CMCNF/CCQDs-5. After two-day moisture equilibration at 25 °C and 50 % RH, the films' mass and thickness were measured using an analytical balance and thickness meter. The quantitative values and tightness are presented in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) patterns were acquired using a JEM 2100F (JEOL, Japan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were acquired using a Multimode 8 AFM (Bruker, Germany). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were acquired using an SU5000 field-emission scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured using an X'pert3 Powder diffractometer (PANalytical, Netherlands) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) at 40 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a TENSOR 27 infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) patterns were obtained using an Axis Supra+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, UK) with Al-Kα radiation. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded using a Cary 60 spectrophotometer (Agilent, US). Photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra were recorded using a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer (HoribaX, France) with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a TG 209 F1 Libra thermogravimetric analyzer (NETZSCH, Germany), heating samples from 30 °C to 800 °C at 10 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. Particle size distribution was determined using an SZ-100Z nanoparticle analyzer (HORIBA, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield (QY) measurement of CCQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (QY) of CCQDs was determined using previously reported methods [39,40]. QY was calculated using Eq. (2), with quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4 as the reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CCQDs for Fe3+ detection
Sensitivity and selectivity of Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the CCQDs/PBS mixture, 400 μL of CCQDs (1 mg/mL) was mixed with 3200 μL of PBS solution (pH 7.2). Then, the following experiments were performed: (1) 400 μL of 1 mM solutions of various metal ions (Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, and Al3+) were individually added to the CCQDs/PBS mixture to assess the selectivity of CCQDs for Fe3+ detection; (2) 400 μL of Fe3+ solutions at varying concentrations were added to the CCQDs/PBS mixture for sensitivity evaluation, without other metal ions; (3) 200 μL of 1 mM Fe3+ solution and 200 μL of 1 mM solutions of other metal ions were simultaneously added to the CCQDs/PBS mixture to analyze interference effects. The fluorescence spectra of each sample were recorded at 360 nm excitation after standing for 10 min. Measurements were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ detection in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Water samples were collected from lake water and internal water of the tofu packaging. Before testing, the samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Then, Fe3+ solutions were added to the samples to achieve concentrations of 0 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM, 40 μM, and 60 μM. The spiked samples were mixed with CCQDs, and their fluorescence spectra were recorded at 360 nm excitation after a 10-min incubation. The mean recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated to evaluate assay accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The films were equilibrated for 2 days under constant temperature and humidity conditions, then cut into 5 cm by 1 cm strips. Their thickness was measured before testing using a universal testing machine, with a tensile speed of 1.3 mm/min and a collet spacing of 1 cm. Each film was tested five times to determine its tensile strength, elastic modulus, and toughness.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photobleaching resistance of CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The photobleaching resistance of CMCNF/CCQDs was evaluated through the following tests: (1) Fluorescence spectra were recorded at 0.5-h intervals under 360 nm UV light irradiation (λex = 360 nm); (2) Fluorescence spectra were recorded at one-month intervals (λex = 360 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the feasibility of CMCNF/CCQDs for Fe3+ detection, 5 μL of Fe3+ solutions at varying concentrations were added dropwise to the films. After drying, fluorescence spectra were measured at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

Lake water and internal water of the tofu packaging were collected for spiking recovery experiments. The water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane before being spiked to prepare solutions with varying Fe3+ concentrations. 5 μL of the spiked water samples were added dropwise to the CMCNF/CCQDs, and fluorescence spectra were measured at 360 nm excitation after drying. The mean recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated to evaluate assay accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization
Characterization of CCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation, filtration, dialysis, and lyophilization processes of CCQDs are illustrated in Fig. S1.

As shown in Table S2, the Cr content of hydrolyzed collagen was 0.00086 %, below the GB 2762–2022 standard limit for food contaminants, achieving a Cr removal rate of 99.93 %. The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of CCQDs increased with reaction time and temperature, peaking at 200 °C and 40 min (Fig. S3). However, further increases in temperature and reaction time reduced the QY (Fig. S2). This decrease is likely due to excessive carbonization and degradation of surface states, which adversely affected fluorescence properties. The CCQDs prepared at 200 °C for 40 min exhibited a QY of 25.04 %, higher than most biomass-based carbon quantum dots, with shortened preparation time (Fig. S4). This suggests that high-QY CQDs can be efficiently synthesized via the microwave hydrothermal method using secondary collagen fibers as carbon and nitrogen sources.

The CCQDs solution appeared transparent dark brown under daylight (Fig. S5) and contained near-spherical particles with diameters ranging from 2 to 15 nm, averaging 8.39 nm (Fig. 1(a)). The HR-TEM image showed distinct lattice stripes in several CCQDs, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, resembling the (100) planes of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1(b)) [41]. XRD patterns showed a diffraction peak with a certain width near 2θ = 22° (d002 = 0.34 nm) and a weaker peak at 2θ = 43° (d001 = 0.21 nm), indicating locally graphitized amorphous structures (Fig. 1(c)). These results suggest the CCQDs consist of carbon cores with conjugated π-bonds and surface functional groups.

Surface functional groups of CCQDs were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1(d). The broad absorption band near 3290 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of NH and -OH. The absorption peaks at 2950 cm−1, 1654 cm−1, 1556 cm−1, 1170 cm−1, 1082 cm−1, and 1028 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CH, CO, aromatic CC, C-N-C, C-O-C, and CO, respectively. The peaks ranging from 1228 cm−1 to 1460 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CN. These results confirm the presence of amino groups on the CCQDs surface, and the doping of amino groups could enhance the fluorescence properties [42–44]. XPS spectra (Fig. 1(e)) showed three major peaks: C1s (281.2 eV), N1s (396.4 eV), and O1s (528.2 eV), with atomic percentages of 80.2 %, 6.1 %, and 13.8 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1(f)) revealed three peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.7 eV, and 288.3 eV, corresponding to CC, C-O/C-N, and CO. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1(g)) showed peaks for pyrrole N (396.6 eV) and NH (398.0 eV) [45]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1(h)) showed two peaks at 528.5 eV and 530.0 eV, corresponding to CO and C-OH/C-O-C [46]. The C1s and N1s spectra confirmed the formation of amino-N and pyrrole-like N in the CCQDs, indicating successful nitrogen doping into the carbon cores. These results were consistent with the FT-IR analysis. FT-IR and XPS spectra analysis indicate that the synthesized CCQDs contain functional groups such as -OH and -NH2. These functional groups formed as a result of collagen decomposition during the microwave hydrothermal reaction, which enhanced the solubility of the CCQDs in water. Additionally, these functional groups may lead to the formation of surface-defective states in the CCQDs, which is favorable for the CCQDs to exhibit strong photoluminescence.

To verify the purification effect of the CCQDs, 1H NMR testing was performed, as described in the literature [47]. The 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S6) of the unpurified CCQDs showed distinct signals in the 1–4 ppm (aliphatic region) and 7–8.5 ppm (aromatic region), indicating the presence of small molecular components. Signals in the 0–1 ppm region are likely attributed to aliphatic groups or residual unreacted precursors. After dialysis with a 1 kDa membrane, some signals were eliminated, and the intensity of the remaining signals decreased, indicating the removal of small molecules and an improvement in sample purity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CMCNF
ce:section.ce:para: Carboxymethylation introduced negatively charged carboxyl groups via nucleophilic substitution, enhancing electrostatic repulsion, reducing intermolecular interactions, and preserving the fiber length. In this study, carboxymethylated nanocellulose (CMCNF) was prepared through carboxymethylation pre-treatment and mechanical homogenization, achieving a carboxyl content of 1.33 mmol/g. As shown in Fig. 2 , the fiber length of CMCNF exceeded 1–2 μm, while the diameter was below 4 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties and stability of CCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of CCQDs were analyzed through UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the UV–vis spectrum of CCQDs exhibited a peak near 242 nm, attributed to π-π* transitions of CC bonds. Additional peaks at approximately 274 nm and 340 nm corresponded to conjugated π-bonding in aromatic rings and n-π* transitions of C=O/C-N and NH chromophores. The CCQDs exhibited optimal excitation and emission peaks at 360 nm and 450 nm, respectively. The inset of Fig. 3(a) shows that CCQDs appeared non-fluorescent in daylight but emitted bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Fig. 3(b) shows the emission spectra of CCQDs under different excitation wavelengths. As the excitation wavelength increased from 360 nm to 410 nm, the emission peaks shifted to longer wavelengths, while their intensity weakened, highlighting excitation wavelength dependence. This phenomenon may result from variations in surface defect states and carbon cores of differing particle sizes.

The fluorescence decay curves of CCQDs (Fig. 3(c)) were fitted with a double-exponential function, yielding an average fluorescence lifetime of 6.09 ns. The short fluorescence lifetime suggests the presence of numerous surface defects in CCQDs, which act as energy traps for radiative recombination and promote fluorescence [48].

The fluorescence intensity of CCQDs was evaluated under different environmental conditions and storage periods (Fig. S7). Under prolonged UV light exposure (λ = 360 nm), the relative fluorescence intensity of CCQDs remained in the range of 0.94 to 0.99, decreasing by only 5.9 % after 5 h (Fig. S7(a)). With increasing NaCl concentration, the fluorescence intensity (λex = 360 nm) of CCQDs remained nearly unchanged (Fig. S7(b)). Over time, the fluorescence intensity of CCQDs decreased slightly, retaining 95.8 % of its initial value after 12 months (Fig. S7(c)). These results suggest that CCQDs exhibit excellent resistance to photobleaching, stability in inorganic salts, and overall fluorescence stability, which are of great significance for practical applications.

The pH effects on CCQDs fluorescence intensity were examined (Fig. 3(d)). The fluorescence intensity decreased significantly under acidic and alkaline conditions, with a more pronounced reduction in alkaline conditions. This was likely caused by deprotonation of chemical bonds, resulting in negatively charged hydroxyl groups on the CCQDs surface, which induced aggregation and fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence intensity remained stable within the pH range of 5–8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ detection performance of CCQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Under UV light, CCQDs solutions with various metal ions exhibited different fluorescence effects (Fig. S8). The addition of Fe3+ ions almost completely quenched the fluorescence, whereas other metal ions caused minimal changes. The integrated fluorescence intensity ratio (I
0
/I) of CCQD solutions, with and without metal ions, is shown in Fig. 4(a). A significant increase in the I
0
/I ratio was observed in the presence of Fe3+, reflecting a marked reduction in fluorescence intensity. In contrast, other metal ions had a negligible impact on the I
0
/I ratio. The positions of the emission peaks for the different CCQDs solutions remained unchanged (Fig. S8). These results demonstrate the selective detection of Fe3+ by CCQDs.

As the Fe3+ concentration increased from 0 μM to 1000 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the CCQD solution gradually decreased (Fig. S9). However, the positions of the emission peaks remained unchanged. A strong linear relationship between I
0
/I and Fe3+ concentration was observed in the range of 0 μM to 180 μM (Fig. 4(b)). The linear equation is as follows: y = 1.0000 + 0.0039 x
R 2
= 0.9866
. The detection limit (LOD) of Fe3+ was calculated to be 3.84 μM using the triple standard deviation method (Eq. (5)). These results demonstrate that CCQDs exhibit high sensitivity for detecting Fe3+, highlighting their potential for broad applications.
(5)
LOD =
3 σ
k
where σ is the relative standard deviation of fluorescence integral intensity values from 10 consecutive measurements of CCQD solutions under identical conditions, and k is the slope of the linear equation.

Table 2 compares the detection limits (LOD), detection ranges, and quantum yields (QY) of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from various biomass sources for Fe3+ detection. Although the LOD of our CCQDs (3.84 μM) is higher than that of some other CQDs, they exhibit a broader linear range (0–180 μM) and a higher quantum yield (25.04 %). These factors enhance the sensitivity and versatility of our CCQDs for Fe3+ detection in practical applications.

The stability of CCQDs for Fe3+ detection was evaluated through anti-interference experiments with other metal ions. In the presence of interfering metal ions (1 mM), Fe3+ still significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity of the CCQDs solution, with minimal change compared to the blank sample. This indicates that coexisting metal ions had a negligible effect on detection (Fig. 4(c)). Temporal stability was also assessed (Fig. 4(d)). The fluorescence of CCQDs was rapidly quenched by Fe3+ and remained stable for two hours. These results demonstrate that CCQDs exhibit excellent anti-interference and stability for Fe3+ detection.

As shown in Table S3, the Fe3+ concentration measured using the linear equations showed strong consistency with actual concentrations, with recoveries ranging from 93.59 % to 105.92 % and relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 2 %. These results demonstrate the high accuracy and reliability of CCQDs for Fe3+ detection in environmental and food packaging water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quenching mechanism of CCQDs for Fe3+ was investigated based on the Stern-Volmer Eq. (6):
(6)
I 0
/ I = 1 +
K SV
Q
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
Q
where I
0 and I are the integrated fluorescence intensities of CCQDs without and with the quencher, respectively, K
SV is the Stern-Volmer constant (L∙mol−1), [Q] is the quencher concentration, k
q is the dynamic quenching rate constant (L∙mol−1∙s−1), and τ
0 is the lifetime of CCQDs without the quencher (s).

When the Fe3+ concentration was below 180 μM, a linear correlation between I
0
/I and Fe3+ was observed (Fig. 4(b)), consistent with the Stern-Volmer equation. This yielded a K
SV value of 3.9 × 103 L/mol and a τ
0 value of 6.03 × 10−9 s. Consequently, a k
q value of 6.468 × 1011 L∙mol−1∙s−1 was calculated, exceeding the typical range for dynamic quenching rate constants (1010 L∙mol−1∙s−1 to 1011 L∙mol−1∙s−1). Therefore, the fluorescence quenching effect can't be attributed to dynamic quenching. The average fluorescence lifetimes of the CCQDs and Fe3+-CCQDs systems were 6.03 ns and 6.38 ns, respectively (Fig. 5(a)), indicating that Fe3+ did not affect the fluorescence lifetime of CCQDs. This further suggests that dynamic quenching and fluorescence resonance energy transfer are not the primary quenching mechanisms. Therefore, it is hypothesized that the quenching mechanism involves static quenching. Fig. 5(b) shows the variation in fluorescence quenching at 25 °C, 40 °C, and 50 °C. The degree of quenching increased with Fe3+ concentration but decreased with temperature, consistent with static quenching. This behavior is due to the weakening of the binding between fluorescent molecules and the quencher as temperature rises, reducing the stability of non-fluorescent complexes and quenching efficiency. After Fe3+ addition, the absorption spectra of CCQDs changed significantly (Fig. 5(c)), with a notable decrease in absorbance at 275 nm and the emergence of a new peak at 300 nm. This change is due to the formation of new non-fluorescent complexes from interactions between Fe3+ and the N and O groups on the CCQDs surface. The overlap of the UV absorption region with the excitation wavelength region is a key feature of the internal filtering effect (IFE). Fig. 5(d) compares the absorption spectra of Fe3+ with the excitation and emission spectra of CCQDs. The overlap of Fe3+ shoulder peaks (300–380 nm) with the excitation peaks of CCQDs suggests that IFE may contribute to fluorescence quenching.

Fig. 5(e) illustrates the proposed fluorescence quenching mechanism of CCQDs by Fe3+. Fe3+ binds to electron-donating groups, such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups, on the CCQD surface. This interaction facilitates electron transfer from the excited-state groups to the d orbitals of Fe3+, forming a nonfluorescent complex via a ligand exchange reaction. The resulting non-radiative recombination of photons leads to efficient fluorescence quenching [60]. The formation of stable chelates between Fe3+ and CCQDs is identified as the primary cause of quenching. The IFE could also contribute to the process. Future studies could employ DFT-based analyses to further explore electronic interactions, providing deeper insights into the quenching mechanism [61,62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The microstructure of the CMCNF/CCQDs was characterized using scanning electron microscopy to observe the cross-sectional morphology (Fig. 6 (m-r)). The cross-section of pure CMCNF films exhibited a tightly layered structure (Fig. 6(m)). When the CCQD content was below 20 %, the particles were well-dispersed during film formation and evenly incorporated into the CMCNF matrix (Fig. 6(n-o)). At CCQD contents between 30 % and 50 %, large aggregates formed, and the cross-sectional morphology became progressively rougher (Fig. 6(p-r)). This was attributed to flocculation during the drying process at high CCQD contents, which disrupted the sensor's ordered structure locally.

Fig. 6(a-l) shows the elemental distribution in the CMCNF/CCQDs-0 and CMCNF/CCQDs-2 cross-sections, as analyzed by energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy. In the cross-section of pure CMCNF films, carbon (C) and oxygen (O) were the predominant elements, with atomic percentages of 63.06 % and 36.94 %, respectively (Fig. 6(f)). In the cross-section of CMCNF/CCQDs-2, the presence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N) was detected, with atomic percentages of 66.42 %, 25.32 %, and 8.25 %, respectively (Fig. 6(l)). The nitrogen primarily originated from the amino groups in the CCQDs structure. The uniform nitrogen distribution in Fig. 6(k) indicates the excellent dispersion of CCQDs within the CMCNF matrix.

FT-IR and XRD analyses were conducted to further characterize the structure of CMCNF/CCQDs (Fig. 6(s-t)). Fig. 6(s) shows that the CCQDs-containing film exhibited a CN peak at 1600 cm−1, confirming the successful incorporation of CCQDs into the CMCNF matrix. The FT-IR spectrum of the CMCNF/CCQDs closely resembled that of pure CMCNF, indicating that the chemical structure of the matrix remained unchanged with CCQDs incorporation. The diffraction patterns of pure CMCNF films displayed a typical cellulose Ιβ structure, with (101) and (002) peaks at 2θ = 14.7° and 22.2°, and a trough at 2θ = 19° corresponding to the amorphous region of cellulose (Fig. 6(t)). With increasing CCQD content, the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 14.7° broadened and weakened. This can be attributed to the decrease in crystal size from CCQDs incorporation, interfacial stresses between the CCQDs and CMCNF, and the non-uniform CCQDs distribution, which causes lattice distortions and peak broadening.

XPS analysis was performed on the CMCNF/CCQDs before and after Fe3+ ion addition (Fig. S10). The binding energy of the CO bond in the C1s spectrum increased from 287.97 eV to 288.15 eV upon Fe3+ introduction, indicating CO coordination with Fe3+. This increase is likely due to Fe3+ reducing the electron cloud density of the oxygen atom, which enhances the CO bond's polarity. In the N1s spectrum, the intensity of the CN bond significantly increased, suggesting strong coordination between Fe3+ and pyridinic nitrogen in the CCQDs. The intensity of the CN bond slightly decreased, possibly due to Fe3+-induced electronic environment changes. A new peak at 529.31 eV in the O1s spectrum, corresponding to FeO bonds, confirms Fe3+ coordination with oxygen-containing functional groups, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. These XPS findings further demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching caused by Fe3+ ions is primarily due to the formation of metal complexes with CCQDs, which alter their electronic structure and energy states, thus suppressing fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transparency of CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Changes in CCQD content altered the optical properties of the CMCNF/CCQDs. Under daylight, the films turned pale yellow with increasing CCQD content (Fig. 7(e)). To understand the appearance changes, the CMCNF/CCQDs were quantitatively analyzed for transmittance and haze. Transmittance reflects the transmission of visible light through the film, while haze represents the light scattered by the film. As shown in Fig. 7(a), transmittance in the blue light range (430–455 nm) decreased with increasing CCQD content, indicating that CCQDs absorbed part of the blue light, causing the composite film to appear yellow. The transmittance of the CMCNF/CCQDs in the UV-A/B light range (275–420 nm) was significantly lower than that of the pure CMCNF film, suggesting that CCQDs provided a shielding effect against UV light. As shown in Fig. 7(c-d), the haze of the films in the visible range first increased and then decreased with increasing CCQD content. At 550 nm, the transmittance of the composite film was greater than 90.16 % (Fig. 7(b)), and the haze was below 5.01 % (Fig. 7(d)), demonstrating high transparency. This suggests that CCQDs had minimal impact on the film's transparency, reflecting their good compatibility and dispersion within the CMCNF matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The CCQD content significantly affected the structural and mechanical properties of the CMCNF/CCQDs. As shown in Fig. 8(a), as the CCQD content increased from 0 wt% to 20 wt%, the tensile strength gradually increased. The tensile strength of CMCNF/CCQDs-2 increased from 171.9 MPa to 208.1 MPa, representing a 21.1 % increase compared to pure CMCNF films. However, further increases in CCQD content led to a significant decrease in tensile strength, from 208.1 MPa (CMCNCF/CCQDs-2) to 50.1 MPa (CMCNF/CCQDs-5), corresponding to a 75.9 % drop. This increase was attributed to hydrogen bonding between CMCNF and electrostatic attraction between the amino groups on CCQDs and the carboxyl groups on CMCNF, which strengthened the films. However, excessive CCQD content led to agglomeration, disrupting interfacial interactions and weakening hydrogen bonding, which rapidly reduced tensile strength. Fig. 8(b) shows a significant increase in the elastic modulus of the CMCNF/CCQDs. The modulus of CMCNF/CCQDs-2 (7.9 GPa) was 125.7 % higher than that of pure CMCNF (3.5 GPa). This enhancement was due to CCQDs limiting slip during tensile testing, restricting elastic deformation, and increasing rigidity. However, Fig. 8(c) shows a rapid decrease in toughness with increasing CCQD content.

Fig. S11 shows the zeta potentials of CMCNF, CCQDs, and CMCNF/CCQDs dispersions. As CCQD content increased, the absolute value of the zeta potential in CMCNF dispersions decreased, indicating a reduction in the dispersion's negative charge. CMCNF, with its negatively charged surface, adsorbs positively charged CCQDs, reducing the surface's negative charge and suggesting a strong interaction between CCQDs and CMCNF. The interaction mechanism between CCQDs and CMCNF is illustrated in Fig. 8(d). Ammonia ions on the CCQDs surface carry a weak positive charge, creating electrostatic attraction with negatively charged carboxyl groups on CMCNF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence performance of CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9(c) shows the composite films under UV light. The pure CMCNF film showed no fluorescence, whereas the CMCNF/CCQDs exhibited distinct blue fluorescence. Fluorescence intensity increased with increasing CCQD content. Fluorescence emission spectra and intensity comparisons of CMCNF/CCQDs are shown in Fig. 9(a-b). The fluorescence emission spectra of CMCNF/CCQDs align with those of the CCQDs solution (Fig. 3(a)), both exhibiting blue fluorescence when excited at 360 nm, with an emission peak at 450 nm. This suggests that CMCNF does not affect the fluorescence properties of the CCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photobleaching resistance of CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The photobleaching resistance of CMCNF/CCQDs are essential for their potential applications. This section examined the changes in fluorescence intensity under prolonged UV irradiation and extended storage time. Fig. 10(a) shows that the relative fluorescence intensity of the CMCNF/CCQDs remained stable between 0.95 and 0.99 under prolonged UV (360 nm) excitation, with only a 4.6 % decrease after 5 h. Fig. 10(b) shows that the fluorescence intensity of the films decreased minimally with extended storage, retaining 97.2 % of its initial intensity after 8 months. These results demonstrate that CMCNF/CCQDs exhibit excellent photobleaching resistance and fluorescence stability, highlighting their potential for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability of CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 shows the effect of CCQD content on film thermal stability. The thermal decomposition onset temperature of the CMCNF/CCQDs was around 280 °C, with decomposition rates decreasing as CCQD content increased. The total residual mass remained relatively unchanged. These results indicate that CMCNF/CCQDs maintain good thermal stability, with no significant effect from increasing CCQD content.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CMCNF/CCQDs thin-film fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the above results, CMCNF/CCQDs-2 was chosen as the optimal film for further evaluation. Fig. 12(c) shows that the fluorescence of CMCNF/CCQDs-2 under UV light decreased as Fe3+ concentration increased. Fig. 12(a) shows the fluorescence spectra of CMCNF/CCQDs-2 quenched by varying Fe3+ concentrations. The fluorescence intensity decreased as Fe3+ concentration increased from 0 μM to 200 μM, with no shift in the emission peaks. Fig. 12(b) shows a strong linear relationship between the integrated fluorescence intensity ratio (I
0
/I) and Fe3+ concentration (0–180 μM), represented by the following equation: y = 0.6496 + 0.3532 x
R 2
= 0.9932
. These results demonstrate the strong sensitivity of CMCNF/CCQDs for Fe3+ detection, supporting the development of portable, rapid detection devices.

To evaluate the practical application of CMCNF/CCQDs, lake water and internal water of the tofu package were spiked separately with Fe3+. As shown in Table S4, CMCNF/CCQDs successfully detected Fe3+ in both samples. The measured concentrations closely matched the actual values, with recoveries ranging from 99.45 % to 105.23 %. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) were below 2 %, demonstrating the accuracy and reliability of the method for detecting Fe3+ in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para:
A portable fluorescent sensor, CMCNF/CCQDs, was successfully fabricated for Fe3+ detection by combining CCQDs and CMCNF. The CCQDs, with a high fluorescence quantum yield (QY = 25.04 %), were synthesized via the microwave hydrothermal method with nitrogen self-doping from secondary collagen fibers. They exhibited a locally graphitized amorphous structure, emitted strong fluorescence under UV excitation at 360 nm, and demonstrated excellent stability. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of Fe3+ on CCQDs likely involves static quenching with a potential contribution from the IFE. The CMCNF/CCQDs demonstrated excellent selectivity for Fe3+ detection with a linear equation of y = 0.6496 + 0.3532 x
R 2
= 0.9932
in the 0–180 μM concentration range. It exhibited reliable detection performance in real water samples, achieving recoveries of 99.45 %–105.23 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 2 %. Moreover, the incorporation of CCQDs modified the structure of the CCQDs/CMCNF composite. When CCQD content was below 20 wt%, the particles were uniformly dispersed. Compared to pure CMCNF film, the tensile strength of CMCNF/CCQDs-2 increased from 171.9 MPa to 208.1 MPa, while the elastic modulus rose from 3.5 GPa to 7.9 GPa. These findings present a simple and eco-friendly solution for portable Fe3+ detection with significant potential applications in environmental monitoring and food packaging.
